hook: The 'Polish Versailles' — King Jan III Sobieski's baroque summer residence that survived every partition, occupation and war intact, with 45 hectares of royal gardens.
facts: Built 1681–1696 for King Jan III Sobieski and Queen Marie Casimire to a design by Augustyn Wincenty Locci; side wings added 1723–1729.
Sobieski acquired the village (then called Milanów) on 23 April 1677 and renamed it Villa Nova / Wilanów.
The palace blends a Polish noble manor, an Italian garden villa and a French Louis XIV palace into one form.
The palace and park ensemble cover about 45 hectares, counted among the foremost baroque gardens in Poland.
It opened as one of Poland's first public museums in 1805 and survived WWII largely undamaged, unlike most of Warsaw.
legend: The palace's facade reliefs and frescoes were conceived as a glorification of Sobieski as a new Roman emperor and victor over the Ottomans at Vienna.
w: The 'Polish Versailles' — Jan III Sobieski's baroque summer palace, raised in the 1680s: part Polish manor, part Italian villa, part Sun King's palace. While Warsaw burned in the war, Wilanów came through almost untouched; it had opened as one of Poland's first public museums in 1805. Forty-five hectares of royal gardens behind it.

hook: Warsaw's 'other' botanical garden — a 40-hectare research garden of the Polish Academy of Sciences on the edge of Las Kabacki, far larger and wilder than the historic UW one.
facts: Founded by decision of the Presidium of the Polish Academy of Sciences in 1974 as a scientific facility for biodiversity conservation.
Stands on the former grounds of a Golf-Klub and the Powsin estate; first plans for a Warsaw botanical garden here date to 1948.
Calls for a Warsaw botanical garden were first raised in 1928 by professors Bolesław Hryniewiecki and Roman Kobendza.
Covers about 40 hectares, of which nearly 30 are open to visitors, with one gate opening directly onto Las Kabacki.
Located at ul. Prawdziwka 2 on the Powsin escarpment in the city's far south.
w: Warsaw's 'other' botanical garden — forty hectares of the Polish Academy of Sciences' research collections on the Powsin escarpment, far bigger than the historic one downtown, with a gate straight onto Las Kabacki. Planned since the 1920s, finally founded in 1974. Roses, rock gardens, an arboretum. The city's southern green edge.

n: Rezerwat przyrody Las Kabacki im. Stefana Starzyńskiego
hook: Warsaw's largest forest reserve — 900+ hectares of old oak-hornbeam woods that a doomed mayor bought for the city's lungs, now bearing his name.
facts: At about 903–925 ha it is the largest contiguous woodland in left-bank Warsaw, roughly 5 km long and 3 km wide.
Once part of the Wilanów estate owned successively by the Sobieski, Potocki, Lubomirski and Branicki families.
Mayor Stefan Starzyński bought the privately-held forest in 1938 to open it to Varsovians for recreation.
Granted nature-reserve status on 11 August 1980; the reserve is named after Starzyński, who was murdered by the Germans in WWII.
Borders the suburban towns of Piaseczno and Konstancin-Jeziorna at the city's southern edge in Ursynów.
legend: Locals long associated the forest with the 1980 LOT Flight 7 crash, which came down nearby and is commemorated within the woods.
w: Warsaw's largest forest — 900 hectares of old oak and hornbeam at the city's southern edge, the lungs that a doomed mayor secured. Stefan Starzyński bought the private woods in 1938 to open them to the people; the Germans murdered him in the war, and the reserve now bears his name. Once a royal hunting ground. Get lost among the oaks.

hook: Warsaw's highest point is a man-made hill of war rubble — topped by a year-round dry ski slope, a chairlift and a 360° view over the flat city.
facts: At 152 m above sea level it is the highest point in Warsaw, rising about 44 m above its surroundings.
Built up from WWII rubble; the rubble heap was covered with soil to form a park around 1967–1970, reaching 138 m.
In 1997 a private investor raised it a further 14 m and added a chairlift and artificial snowmaking, turning it into a ski hill.
The ski run is about 227 m long and operates year-round, with treated surfaces allowing skiing even in summer.
An observation deck at the summit gives panoramic views across the whole of Warsaw.
legend: Like several Warsaw 'mountains', it is essentially a barrow over the pulverised remains of the wartime city — a hill made of the old city itself.
w: Warsaw's highest point isn't natural — a 152-metre hill heaped from the rubble of the destroyed city, greened over in the 1960s and then piled higher in 1997 for a ski slope. A chairlift runs up it; the dry run works even in summer. From the deck on top, the whole flat city spreads out. A mountain built from ruins.

hook: Ursynów's oldest and most beloved hill — a mound an engineer improvised from construction spoil so it wouldn't have to be hauled away, now the district's sledding and sunset spot.
facts: Created in the 1970s during the building of Ursynów's first estate, Koński Jar, from earth dug for blocks and roads plus broken prefab panels.
It is Ursynów's oldest artificial hill and its highest artificial peak, at about 118 m above sea level.
Named after engineer Henryk Cwil (1920–1990), chief inspector of the Stołeczna Dyrekcja Inwestycji Spółdzielczych, who proposed dumping the spoil on-site.
The district officially named the mound after Cwil following his death in 1990.
It sits in the 11.6-hectare Park im. Romana Kozłowskiego, between Surowieckiego street, Dolina Służewiecka and KEN avenue.
legend: Generations of Ursynów children treat it as a real mountain — it features in the local mythology of the sci-fi-looking 'wielka płyta' housing estate immortalised in the TV series Alternatywy 4.
w: Ursynów's beloved hill, and an accident of thrift — when the district's first estate went up in the 1970s, an engineer named Cwil had the dug earth and broken panels dumped on the spot rather than carted off. The mound stuck, and took his name. Now the district sleds and watches the sunset from it. A hill made of leftovers.

hook: Wilanów's wild twin — a riverine forest reserve in the fork of the Wilanówka, hiding the ivy-covered ruins of a romantic rotunda palace named for a grandson.
facts: Lies in the forks of the Wilanówka river and the Sobieski Canal, taking in the northern part of Lake Wilanów.
Under Jan III Sobieski it was a royal hunting ground stocked with bison, hares and fallow deer.
Stanisław Kostka Potocki turned the wilds into a romantic park and named it Morysin after his grandson Maurycy ('Moryś').
In 1811 a small palace with a rotunda modelled on the Temple of Vesta in Tivoli was built here by Potocki and Christian P. Aigner.
Protected as a nature reserve since 1996, covering about 53 ha; its ruined rotunda, keeper's house and wooden gamekeeper's lodge still survive.
legend: The crumbling, vine-wrapped rotunda standing alone among wild trees gives Morysin a reputation as Wilanów's haunted, melancholy back-garden.
w: Wilanów's wild twin — a riverine forest in the fork of the Wilanówka, once Sobieski's bison-stocked hunting ground. Stanisław Kostka Potocki made it a romantic park, named it for his grandson Moryś, and set a rotunda palace in it in 1811, modelled on the Temple of Vesta at Tivoli. Its ivy-covered ruins still stand. A reserve gone gloriously to seed.

hook: A Tsarist brick-and-earth fort ringed by a moat — built to defend Warsaw, later an interwar munitions works and Uprising battlefield, now a tree-shaded park.
facts: Built 1886–1890 as part of the second inner ring of the Warsaw Fortress, with earthen ramparts and a moat still intact.
Originally named Fort 'P' (Parysów) after the nearby village of Parysów; renamed Fort Bema in 1921 after General Józef Bem.
Housed Poland's first ammunition factory between 1924 and 1939.
Taken over by the Germans in WWII for weapon storage and vehicle workshops; site of fierce fighting in the 1944 Warsaw Uprising.
Bemowo district took over the grounds in 1999 and revitalised them from 2002 into a park with footbridges, trails and a wildlife-rich moat.
w: A Tsarist fort of the 1880s on Bemowo, its brick redoubt still ringed by a moat — built to defend Warsaw, turned into Poland's first ammunition factory between the wars, fought over in the 1944 Uprising. The district made a park of it in the 2000s, footbridges over the old moat and wildlife in the water. Guns and powder, gone to green.

hook: Poland's 58,000-seat national stadium, built for Euro 2012 atop the earthen ramparts of the demolished communist-era Stadion Dziesięciolecia and its sprawling Jarmark Europa bazaar.
facts: Built 2008–2011 for nearly two billion złoty as a co-host venue for UEFA Euro 2012, officially opened 29 January 2012.
Holds 58,580 seats for sport (up to ~72,900 for concerts) and was awarded UEFA's top category-four classification.
Constructed on the earthen embankments of the demolished Stadion Dziesięciolecia (1955), whose terraces had become the vast Jarmark Europa open-air market in the 1990s.
Named after Kazimierz Górski, the most celebrated coach of the Polish national football team.
Its retractable roof, a fabric membrane drawn to a central spire, is among the design's signature features.
w: Poland's 58,000-seat national stadium, built for Euro 2012 — and built on a ghost. Its earthen ramparts are the old communist Stadion Dziesięciolecia, whose terraces had become Jarmark Europa, Europe's vast open-air bazaar, in the 1990s. A woven roof draws to a central spire. Football, where the market used to sprawl.

hook: Oskar and Zofia Hansen's serpentine 1970s housing megastructure — 23 prefab blocks chained by 1.5 km of access galleries, nicknamed 'Pekin' by its residents.
facts: Designed by Oskar Hansen and Zofia Garlińska-Hansen and built 1969–1974 in the Praga-Południe district.
Comprises 23 four- and eight-storey large-panel residential blocks linked by galleries totalling about 1.5 km in length.
Residents call the huge galleried structure 'Pekin' (Beijing).
It was the first realisation of the Hansens' 'Linear Continuous System' (LSC), a utopian vision of city-bands stretching across all of Poland.
The estate drew notice in architecture journals in the USSR, Finland, the USA and Japan.
w: Oskar and Zofia Hansen's serpentine megastructure of the 1970s — 23 prefab blocks chained by a kilometre and a half of access galleries, which residents took to calling 'Pekin.' It realised the one built fragment of their wild 'Linear Continuous System,' a dream of housing-bands across all Poland. Utopia, in concrete panels.

hook: The 1831 redoubt immortalised in Mickiewicz's poem 'Reduta Ordona' — long thought lost, then rediscovered and excavated between 2010 and 2013 with the war dead still in the ground.
facts: Redoubt no. 54 of the November Uprising's western fortification belt, defending the approaches to Warsaw in September 1831.
Its defence and blowing-up were immortalised in Adam Mickiewicz's poem 'Reduta Ordona', based on an eyewitness account by Stefan Garczyński.
Archaeological work from October 2010 to 2013 confirmed the site and uncovered soldiers' burials, identified by uniform buttons as Polish 1st Rifle Infantry and several Russian regiments.
Excavators determined the redoubt was a roughly six-sided earthwork about 200 m across, surrounded by a moat for all-round defence.
It lies on ul. Na Bateryjce near the Blue City mall, on the Ochota–Wola border, and was entered in the heritage register in 2016.
legend: Per Mickiewicz, the redoubt's young commander Konstanty Ordon detonated the powder magazine rather than surrender — though the real Ordon in fact survived the war by decades.
w: The redoubt from Mickiewicz's poem — Ordon's earthwork no. 54, which held Warsaw's western approach in 1831 and went up in a famous blast. Long thought lost under the city, it was found and dug from 2010, the dead still in the ground, named by their uniform buttons. A poem, made real again.

hook: The monument and mass grave on the edge of the Olszynka Grochowska reserve, marking the bloodiest battle of the 1831 November Uprising — fought in a frozen alder grove.
facts: Marks and overlies the common grave of those who fell in the Battle of Olszynka Grochowska on 25 February 1831, the largest engagement of the November Uprising.
A wooden memorial cross designed by architect Stefan Szyller was raised on the battlefield on 13 July 1916.
The cross was replaced by a metal one in 1932, with a crypt added in 1936 holding remains found during land-reclamation work.
It stands at the corner of ul. Szeroka and ul. Tracza, on the edge of the Olszynka Grochowska nature reserve in Praga-Południe.
A 2012 proposal envisaged a 50-metre mound here topped with the original shafts of Warsaw's Sigismund's Column, reached by an 'Avenue of Glory'.
w: A cross and a mass grave on the edge of an alder grove in Praga — the resting place of those who fell at Olszynka Grochowska in February 1831, the bloodiest battle of the November Uprising, fought hand to hand in the frozen alders. A wooden cross went up in 1916, a crypt of bones in 1936. Where a rising bled.

hook: One of Europe's largest cemeteries — roughly 1.2 million burials over 113 hectares behind a 5 km wall, the democratic working-class necropolis of right-bank Warsaw since 1884.
facts: Consecrated on 20 November 1884 by Warsaw archbishop Wincenty Teofil Popiel, with the first burial the next day of a one-year-old, Maria Skibniewska.
Covers about 113.3 hectares with a perimeter wall around 5 km long, between the Targówek and Bródno districts at ul. św. Wincentego 83.
With an estimated 1.2 million interred, it is one of the largest cemeteries in Europe by number of burials.
The city bought 65 hectares from the Hospital of the Holy Spirit after the older Kamionkowski cemetery filled up in 1883.
It contains two churches: the wooden church of St Vincent de Paul and the brick church of Our Lady of Częstochowa.
w: One of the largest cemeteries in Europe — 1.2 million graves over 113 hectares behind a five-kilometre wall, right-bank Warsaw's working-class necropolis since 1884. The first grave here held a one-year-old child. Democratic in death, vast beyond reckoning. A city of the dead, in Bródno.

hook: Poland's biggest antiques and flea market — a weekend 'Persian fair' of paintings, glass, junk and treasure on the very Wola fields where the nobility once gathered to elect kings.
facts: Located at ul. Obozowa 99 in the Koło neighbourhood of Wola, in the quadrangle of Obozowa, Ciołka, Księcia Janusza and Newelska streets.
The antiques market runs at weekends; the bazaar also hosts a Monday animal exchange and fruit-and-vegetable trading on other days.
Established in the 1980s, it descends from a 1970s 'Persian fair' first held on Mariensztat.
It is described as the largest and most popular antiques market in all of Poland, drawing tourists as well as Varsovians.
It occupies former Wola election fields, where Polish nobility once camped for weeks during royal elections.
w: Poland's biggest flea and antiques market — a weekend 'Persian fair' of paintings, glass, militaria and junk, sprawled across a corner of Wola. It grew from a 1970s fair and draws diggers from all over. Fittingly, it sits on the old fields where nobles once camped to elect a king. Haggle where kings were made.

hook: Halina Skibniewska's 1970s 'Master of Warsaw' estate beside the Łazienki canal — sculpture-strewn modernist housing built over the parade ground of Piłsudski's chevau-légers.
facts: Built 1972–1974 to a design by architect Halina Skibniewska, whose scheme won first prize in a 1965 SARP competition.
Seven multi-storey blocks in articulated 'Ż' large-block technology house about 1,700 people on roughly 4.5 hectares.
It was awarded the title 'Mister Warszawy' (Master of Warsaw) in 1974.
Sited between Czerniakowska, Szwoleżerów and Dragonów streets and the Piaseczyński Canal in the Ujazdów area of Śródmieście.
The estate is set off by 18th- and 19th-century sculptures brought from Lower Silesia and carefully composed greenery.
It stands on land that was successively a tsarist uhlan barracks, then the barracks of Marshal Piłsudski's chevau-légers (szwoleżerowie).
w: Halina Skibniewska's 'Master of Warsaw' estate of 1974, by the Łazienki canal — modernist blocks strewn with old sculptures brought up from Silesia, set in composed greenery. Humane prefab, like her Sady Żoliborskie. It stands on the old parade ground of Piłsudski's chevau-légers, the szwoleżerowie. Concrete, with statues.

hook: The tractor works that gave a whole Warsaw district its name — a 30,000-strong industrial giant whose workers helped ignite the June 1976 protests against the communist regime.
facts: Founded in 1893, the firm took the name URSUS in 1907 and began mass tractor production in the 1920s.
On 25 June 1976 its workers struck against food-price hikes; the Radom–Ursus protests were a key precursor to Solidarity.
At its peak the plant employed tens of thousands (about 23,400 workers in 1990) and turned out up to 50,000 vehicles a year.
Crippled by debt from a 1970s Massey Ferguson licence deal, ZPC Ursus went bankrupt in 2003 and most of its Warsaw halls were sold off or demolished.
Sited around ul. Traktorzystów in the Ursus district, the works gave the surrounding district its name.
w: The tractor works that named a whole district — founded in 1893, churning out up to 50,000 vehicles a year at its peak. In June 1976 its workers walked out over food prices; the Radom–Ursus protests helped light Solidarity's fuse. Bankrupt by 2003, most of its halls now gone. A factory that shook a regime.
